---
title: "A composable ecosystem for infectious disease modelling"
description: "Why we are building EpiAware, and where it is today."
author: "Sam Abbott"
date: "2026-07-10"
categories: [announcement]
---

Infectious disease models turn data into evidence for decisions, both in routine surveillance and during outbreaks.
For that evidence to be useful it has to be timely, rigorous, and collaborative.
Today's approaches struggle to be all three at once, and closing that gap is what EpiAware is for.

## Why composability

There are two common ways to build these models, and each gives something up.
Chaining separate models together is flexible, but passing summary estimates from one stage to the next loses information and can introduce bias.
Fitting one joint model keeps the statistics honest, but the result is usually monolithic, built for a single problem, and hard to adapt or share.
So when a new outbreak needs different expertise, teams tend to rebuild from scratch rather than reuse what already exists.

Composable models are a way to keep the rigour of a joint model while regaining the flexibility of separate ones.
If a model is assembled from interchangeable, reusable parts, a domain expert can contribute one component without having to understand the whole system.
Shared components also let different groups trace their differences to assumptions rather than implementation details.
This is the argument set out in the epiforecasts modelling workflow and the composable modelling grant, and EpiAware is where we are putting it into practice in Julia.

## What EpiAware is

EpiAware is a set of small, interoperable Julia packages, not a single framework.
We are not yet sure which way of composing models will work best, so we are trying more than one and keeping them interoperable.
Some parts build on [Turing.jl](https://turinglang.org) for full Bayesian inference; others extend [Distributions.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStats/Distributions.jl) and are useful on their own.
The [approaches](/approaches/index.qmd) page describes the routes we are exploring so far.

- **Aim**: interchangeable components that combine into joint models, propagate uncertainty properly, and make surveillance and outbreak workflows quicker to build and adapt.
- **Approaches**: more than one route to composition, kept interoperable, growing as we learn what works.
- **Alongside**: other efforts with the same goal — [DDSALineLists.jl](https://github.com/epirecipes/DDSALineLists.jl), [CategoricalOutbreaks.jl](https://github.com/epirecipes/CategoricalOutbreaks.jl), and [EpiBranch.jl](https://epiforecasts.io/EpiBranch.jl/dev/).

## Where it is today

[CensoredDistributions.jl](/packages/censoreddistributions.qmd) is the most developed.
The joint-modelling and composition packages are in active development.
Each package's page says how settled it is.

## Get involved

Collaborators welcome, whether that is code, docs, examples, or a modelling problem to test the approach on.
See [Get involved](/get-involved.qmd), or start a conversation on the [epinowcast forum](https://community.epinowcast.org/).
